rationale: Reducible error and irreducible error are the two mutually exclusive components of expected prediction error; reducible error can be decreased by improving the model, while irreducible error is due to inherent noise and cannot be eliminated.
The bias-variance tradeoff explains that overfitting corresponds to high variance in the decomposition, clarifying why overly flexible models generalize poorly.
Training error is computed on the same data used for fitting and is optimistically biased; test error is computed on new, unseen data and is the primary measure of generalization performance.
Simple linear regression is typically fitted via least squares estimation; without understanding least squares, one cannot fully grasp how coefficient estimates are obtained.
Logistic regression models the log-odds (logit) as a linear function of predictors; the logit link is fundamental to the model specification.
Understanding odds and odds ratios strengthens the interpretation of logistic regression coefficients as multiplicative effects on the odds.

LDA is a generative classifier that models the joint distribution of predictors and response, while logistic regression is a discriminative classifier that directly models the conditional probability; they offer different perspectives and assumptions.
QDA generalizes LDA by allowing each class to have its own covariance matrix; understanding the common covariance assumption of LDA is necessary to appreciate QDA's flexibility.
Logistic regression is a specific member of the GLM family with a logit link and binomial distribution; GLM concepts are foundational for a structured understanding.
Poisson regression is a GLM with a log link and Poisson distribution; GLM concepts provide the framework for understanding its structure.
LOOCV uses n-1 observations for training and has low bias but high variance and computational cost; k-fold CV with k < n typically has lower variance and higher bias, illustrating a tradeoff.

Bootstrap samples with replacement to create multiple datasets of size n, while k-fold CV splits the data into non-overlapping folds without replacement; they serve different purposes in resampling and error estimation.

Best subset selection exhaustively evaluates all 2^p possible models, while forward stepwise is a greedy search that adds predictors sequentially; they differ in optimality and computational feasibility.

Forward selection starts with the null model and adds predictors, while backward selection starts with the full model and removes predictors; they are complementary greedy strategies with different requirements (n > p for backward).
AIC uses a penalty of 2 on the number of parameters, while BIC uses log(n); BIC penalizes complexity more heavily, leading to the selection of simpler models for large n.

Ridge regression uses an L2 penalty that shrinks coefficients but cannot set them to zero, while lasso uses an L1 penalty that can perform variable selection by forcing some coefficients to exactly zero.

PCR uses unsupervised PCA for dimension reduction, ignoring the response, while PLS uses supervised components that maximize covariance with the response; this leads to different predictive performance.

The curse of dimensionality explains why KNN becomes ineffective in high dimensions, as data points become sparse and distances lose meaning, strengthening the need for dimensionality reduction or alternative methods.

Polynomial regression uses a single global polynomial, which can exhibit extreme behavior at boundaries, while regression splines use piecewise polynomials with local flexibility and continuity constraints.
Smoothing splines are a penalized version of regression splines that automatically place knots at data points; understanding regression splines provides the basis for the penalized approach.
Generalized additive models often use smoothing splines to model the unknown smooth functions for each predictor, so understanding smoothing splines strengthens GAM interpretation.
A regression tree is a specific type of decision tree for a quantitative response; the general concepts of recursive splitting and leaf-based prediction are prerequisites.
A classification tree is a decision tree for a qualitative response; the general tree structure and splitting framework are necessary foundations.
Cost complexity pruning provides a method to reduce overfitting in decision trees by penalizing tree size; it is a key technique for improving tree generalization.
Random forests extend bagging by randomly selecting a subset of predictors at each split to decorrelate the trees; understanding bagging is necessary to appreciate this extension.

Bagging builds many trees independently on bootstrap samples and averages them to reduce variance, while boosting sequentially grows trees on residuals and combines them with shrinkage; they have different bias-variance tradeoffs.
The support vector classifier (soft margin) generalizes the maximal margin classifier by allowing some margin violations; understanding the hard-margin case is a foundation.
The support vector machine extends the linear support vector classifier by using kernels to create non-linear decision boundaries; the linear classifier concept is a prerequisite.
Kernels are central to the SVM's ability to handle non-linear data; without understanding kernels, one cannot grasp how the SVM achieves non-linear classification.
A CNN is a specialized neural network architecture for spatial data; knowledge of basic neural networks (layers, activations, training) is required to understand CNNs.
An RNN is a neural network architecture for sequences; core neural network concepts are necessary to understand recurrent connections and shared weights.
Backpropagation is the algorithm used to compute gradients for training neural networks; a deep understanding of neural network training requires knowledge of backpropagation.
SGD and its variants are the primary optimization methods for neural networks; understanding SGD strengthens comprehension of how neural networks are trained in practice.
Dropout is a widely used regularization technique for neural networks that prevents co-adaptation; it enhances understanding of how overfitting is mitigated in deep models.
The Kaplan-Meier estimator is specifically designed to estimate the survival function from censored data; understanding censoring is necessary to appreciate why and how the estimator works.
The Kaplan-Meier estimator provides a non-parametric estimate of the survival function; one must know what the survival function is to understand what is being estimated.
The Cox regression model directly models the hazard function; understanding the hazard function is essential to interpret the model's output and proportional hazards assumption.
PCR first applies PCA to the predictors and then uses the principal components in a linear model; understanding PCA is a necessary first step.

K-means requires a pre-specified number of clusters K and partitions observations into flat clusters, while hierarchical clustering builds a dendrogram without a pre-set K, offering a different view of cluster relationships.
The Bonferroni correction is a method to control the FWER; understanding the concept of FWER is necessary to understand the goal and mechanism of the correction.
The Benjamini-Hochberg procedure is designed to control the FDR; knowing what FDR represents is a prerequisite for appreciating why the procedure works.
FWER controls the probability of making any false positive (P(V ≥ 1)), while FDR controls the expected proportion of false positives among all rejections; they address different error control goals.
VIF is a diagnostic measure that quantifies the severity of multicollinearity for a given predictor; understanding multicollinearity is necessary to comprehend what VIF measures.
